overview.body: Remember writing if choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y': over and over? Those days are OVER. This week you'll discover .upper() and .strip() — string methods that clean and normalize input in one line. You'll also add hazards loaded from a data file and a D20 travel system where dice rolls determine how far you go each turn.

mentorQuote.quote: I spent SO much time writing if choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y' everywhere. When someone showed me choice.upper() == 'Y', I was like... wait, that’s IT? One method fixes ALL my case problems? Now I .upper() or .lower() every string comparison!

objectives: Write load_hazards() to parse hazards.txt into a list of dictionaries
Write handle_hazard() to pick a random hazard and flip a coin for the outcome
Write travel_to() with D20-based progression and a 30% hazard chance per turn
Track hazards faced and escaped in the final trip report
overview.keyConceptsText:
Chapter 8: .upper(), .lower(), .strip(), and method chaining
Chapter 9: List of dictionaries, random.choice() with a list of dicts
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapters 8 & 9
programRequirements: Your program MUST use .strip().upper() for all yes/no input handling
Your program MUST load hazards from hazards.txt into a list of dictionaries
Your program MUST use D20 rolls for travel distance (roll × 10 miles per turn)
Your program MUST track and display hazards faced and escaped in the final report
overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-2.py and your function_library.py this week. Start by copying your lab-4-1.py and renaming it.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep lab-4-2.py — Module 4.3 adds sets and a visited-cities tracker directly on top of this foundation.

dataFiles.format: Your shared function library from Module 3

overview.additionalFilesNote: CodeGrade tests your functions in isolation, so both files must be present.

timeGuide.strategy: This is an ENHANCE week — you already have the dictionary foundation from 4.1. Focus on getting .strip().upper() working everywhere before adding hazards and travel.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you're adding. [REPLACED] tags show what changes from your 4.1 code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- DATA LOADING FUNCTIONS ----
load_city_distances()   reads city_distances.txt → nested dict
load_city_info()        reads city_info.txt → dict of dicts
load_hazards()          reads hazards.txt → list of dicts     [NEW]

# ---- GAME FUNCTIONS ----
display_destinations()  shows menu from dict
get_destination_choice()  validates user pick
handle_hazard(hazards)  random hazard + coin flip              [NEW]
travel_to(distance)     D20 travel + hazard checks             [NEW]

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----
Load data files (distances, info, hazards)
Get player name
current_city = 'San Antonio'

GAME LOOP (each pass is one leg):
│
│── Display city info, show destinations
│── Get choice with .strip().upper()          [REPLACED] was or chains
│── Destination Gate (coin flip)
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP (D20 rolls!):               [REPLACED] was fixed 50-mile
│   │── Roll D20, move (roll × 10) miles
│   │── 30% chance: handle_hazard()           [NEW]
│   │── Repeat until you arrive
│
│── Update trip totals (+ hazards_faced, hazards_escaped)
│── Check outcome: beach / dead end / continue
│── Save and quit option

# ---- AFTER THE LOOP ----
Print trip summary with hazard stats          [NEW]
"Thanks for playing!"

checkpoints.title: Copy & Load Hazards

content.body: A function that reads hazards.txt and returns a list of dictionaries. Each hazard dictionary has keys for name, heads message, and tails message.

Loading structured data from files into a list of dictionaries is everywhere in professional development. JSON APIs return lists of objects, database queries return lists of records, and configuration files store lists of settings. Master this pattern and you've unlocked how real applications manage data.

references.description: Your load_city_info() from Lab 4.1 already creates dictionaries from CSV lines. This is the same pattern — but you'll append each hazard dict to a list instead of adding it to a parent dict.

items: Copy your lab-4-1.py and rename it to lab-4-2.py.
Update your docstring description to reflect Lab 4.2.
Define load_hazards(filename) that takes a filename parameter.
Create an empty list inside the function: hazards = [].
Open the file and loop through each line.
For each line: use line.strip().split(',') to get a list of parts.
Create a hazard dict: {'name': parts[0], 'heads': parts[1], 'tails': parts[2]}.
Append the dict to the list: hazards.append(hazard).
Close the file and return the hazards list.

content.body: Step 1: Add this test harness:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
hazards = load_hazards('hazards.txt')
print(f'Loaded {len(hazards)} hazards')
print('First hazard:', hazards[0])
print('First hazard name:', hazards[0]['name'])

content.body: Step 2: Run and verify this output:
Step 3: DELETE the DEBUG lines before moving to Checkpoint 2.

content.quote: You've been writing 'if x == y or x == Y:' for weeks. Today that pain ends. You're also loading hazards from a file and building a real travel system with D20 rolls. Three new functions, one massive quality-of-life upgrade.

rows: Using wrong index: parts[3]
Only 3 parts per line (indices 0, 1, 2). Check your CSV structure.
Forgetting .strip() on the line
Trailing newlines corrupt your data and comparisons will fail.
Creating the dict outside the loop
Each line needs a NEW dict. Create it inside the loop.

content.body:
• lab-4-2.py with an updated docstring
  • load_hazards() that returns a list of dictionaries
  • Each dict has 'name', 'heads', and 'tails' keys
  • Can access hazards with hazards[0]['name']

checkpoints.subtitle: Start from your Lab 4.1 code and add a new file-loading function for hazards.

checkpoints.title: The .upper() Relief

content.body: Clean, normalized input handling using .strip().upper(). No more if x == 'y' or x == 'Y':.

Input normalization is one of the first things real applications need. Every web form, every API, every command-line tool deals with the reality that users don't type consistently. .strip().upper() is the chapter-level introduction to a principle: clean your input at the boundary before your logic ever touches it. One method chain does what four or conditions used to do.

references.description: Shows .upper() and .lower() being called on strings. The comparison pattern if user_input.upper() == 'Y': is exactly what you are implementing here.
You used .split('/') on a string. .strip() and .upper() are from the same string method family — you are just calling them in a chain now.

items: Find the dead-end handling in your game loop (the "Return to San Antonio?" input).
Change the input line to use method chaining: choice = input('Return to San Antonio? (y/n): ').strip().upper().
Update the comparison: check for 'Y' only, not 'y'.
Update the player name input to use .strip() to remove accidental leading or trailing spaces.
Search the rest of your code for any remaining or patterns in input handling and fix them the same way.

content.title: Old Way vs. New Way
content.body: Here's the before and after:
items: # Old: if choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y':
# New: choice = input(...).strip().upper() then if choice == 'Y':

content.body: Run your game and navigate to a dead end (Laredo or El Paso). When asked "Return to San Antonio?", try each of these inputs and confirm all three work: y, Y, and   y   (with spaces).

content.output:
Return to San Antonio? (y/n): y
Heading back to San Antonio...

Return to San Antonio? (y/n): Y
Heading back to San Antonio...

Return to San Antonio? (y/n):    y   
Heading back to San Antonio...

content.quote: Players will do exactly what you didn't expect. They'll type 'Y', 'y', 'YES', or ' y ' with extra spaces. .strip().upper() handles ALL of that in one line. Design for chaos.

rows: Still checking lowercase after .upper()
Only compare against 'Y' and 'N', never 'y' or 'n'.
Forgetting the parentheses: .upper
Methods require parentheses: .upper().
Reversed order: .upper().strip()
Won't crash, but .strip().upper() is the standard pattern. Strip the junk first, then transform.

content.body:
• All yes/no inputs use .strip().upper()
  • No remaining or patterns for case handling
  • Player name input uses .strip()

checkpoints.subtitle: Replace those painful or statements with clean string methods!

checkpoints.title: Handle Hazards

content.body: A function that selects a random hazard from your list, displays it, flips a coin, and shows the appropriate outcome message. Returns True if the player escaped (HEADS) or False if not (TAILS).

random.choice() on a list of dictionaries is the real-world pattern for selecting from structured data. Every game engine, every recommendation system, every A/B testing framework runs on some version of 'pick a random item from a structured list and act on its properties.' Your hazards list is a tiny database and handle_hazard() is your first query.

content.quote: I test this stuff for a living. Random hazard selection sounds simple until you realize you have to verify that EVERY hazard can appear, BOTH coin outcomes work, and the return value is always a boolean. Test it in a loop, run it twenty times, make sure nothing surprises you.

references.description: You built a dictionary with key-value pairs and accessed values by key. handle_hazard() does the same: hazard['name'], hazard['heads'], hazard['tails'] — same pattern, different data.
Shows a function that returns a calculated value. handle_hazard() returns a boolean — same return statement pattern, just returning True or False instead of a number.

items: Define handle_hazard(hazards) that takes the hazards list.
Pick a random hazard: hazard = random.choice(hazards).
Display the hazard name: *** HAZARD: {hazard['name']} ***.
Flip a coin: result = function_library.flip_coin().
Display the flip result.
If HEADS: print hazard['heads'] and return True.
If TAILS: print hazard['tails'] and return False.

content.body: Step 1: Add this test harness:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 4
hazards = load_hazards('hazards.txt')
print('Testing hazard handling...')
for i in range(3):
    escaped = handle_hazard(hazards)
    print(f'Escaped: {escaped}')
    print()

content.body: Step 2: Run several times. Verify you see different hazards and both HEADS and TAILS outcomes appearing across runs. Step 3: DELETE the DEBUG lines before moving to Checkpoint 4.

content.output:
Testing hazard handling...
*** HAZARD: Buc-ee's appears! ***
Coin flip: HEADS
You resist the siren call of Buc-ee's and stay on schedule!
Escaped: True

*** HAZARD: Speed trap! ***
Coin flip: TAILS
You lose 30 minutes to a speed trap!
Escaped: False

*** HAZARD: Armadillo crossing! ***
Coin flip: HEADS
The armadillo waddles off the road. You're clear!
Escaped: True

rows: Using hazard['HEADS'] (uppercase key)
Keys are lowercase: 'heads' and 'tails', matching how you defined them in load_hazards().
Forgetting to return True or False
The caller needs to know if the player escaped. The function must return a boolean.
Using random.choice(hazards) without import random
Make sure import random is at the top of your file.

content.body:
• A working handle_hazard(hazards) that returns True or False
  • Random hazard selection using random.choice()
  • Correct lowercase dictionary keys: 'heads' and 'tails'
  • DEBUG test lines deleted

checkpoints.subtitle: Create a function that picks a random hazard and determines the outcome with a coin flip.

checkpoints.title: D20 Travel System

content.body: A travel_to() function where you roll a D20 each turn, travel roll × 10 miles, and have a 30% chance of encountering a hazard. The journey continues until you've covered the full distance to the destination.

The travel system is where everything converges — the D20 roll from the library, the hazard system from CP3, the probability check from Module 2. Building it as a standalone function that returns a tuple keeps main() clean and readable. Tuple returns are how Python passes multiple values back from a function — a pattern you will use constantly in real code.

content.quote: travel_to() is the most complex function you have written yet — a loop, a probability check, a function call inside a conditional, and a tuple return. Build it one line at a time. Get the D20 loop working first, then add the hazard check, then add the return. Don't try to write it all at once.

references.description: You wrote a while loop that continued until a condition was met. The travel loop — while remaining > 0: — is the same structure: keep going until the distance is covered.
You wrote a function that returned a calculated value. travel_to() returns a tuple of three values — same return statement, just with parentheses around multiple items.

items: Define travel_to(destination, distance, hazards).
Initialize: remaining = distance, miles_traveled = 0, hazards_faced = 0, hazards_escaped = 0.
Create the travel loop: while remaining > 0:.
Roll the D20: roll = function_library.roll_d20().
Calculate miles this turn: miles = roll * 10.
Cap miles if it would overshoot the destination: if miles > remaining: miles = remaining.
Update accumulators: add miles to miles_traveled, subtract from remaining.
Print progress: show the roll, miles gained this turn, and remaining distance.
Check for a hazard only if remaining > 0: use function_library.check_percentage(30).
If a hazard triggers: call handle_hazard(hazards), increment hazards_faced, and increment hazards_escaped if it returned True.
After the loop, return the tuple: (miles_traveled, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped).

content.title: No Hazards on the Last Turn
content.body: Only check for hazards when remaining > 0. You don't want a hazard to fire on the turn you've already arrived at your destination.
Update main() to use the new system:

items: Load hazards alongside city data at the start of main().
Remove the old coin flip destination gate (the D20 travel system replaces it).
Call travel_to() after the player chooses a destination, passing destination name, distance, and hazards list.
Unpack the returned tuple and add to running totals: miles, faced, escaped = travel_to(...).
Update the final report to display total hazards faced and escaped.

content.body: Play through the complete game and verify: D20 rolls determine progress (roll × 10 miles), hazards appear randomly on about 30% of turns, hazard outcomes depend on the coin flip, the final report shows hazards escaped vs. faced, and total miles matches the sum of all journeys.

content.output:
--- Traveling to Houston (200 miles) ---
Turn 1: Rolled 14 — traveled 140 miles. (60 remaining)
*** HAZARD: Speed trap! ***
Coin flip: TAILS
You lose 30 minutes to a speed trap!
Turn 2: Rolled 7 — traveled 60 miles. (0 remaining)
You have arrived in Houston!

--- TRIP SUMMARY ---
Total Miles: 200
Hazards Faced: 1
Hazards Escaped: 0

rows: Using check_percentage(0.3)
The function expects a whole number: check_percentage(30).
D20 range wrong: randint(0, 20)
A D20 rolls 1–20, not 0–20. Use the library function to be safe.
Forgetting to return the tuple
The caller needs (miles, faced, escaped) to update the final report.

content.body:
• travel_to() with D20-based progression and a 30% hazard chance per turn
  • Returns a tuple of (miles_traveled, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped)
  • Updated main() that integrates all new features
  • Final report includes hazard statistics

checkpoints.subtitle: Replace instant travel with dice-roll-based progression and random hazard encounters.

beforeYouSubmit: Docstring updated with name, abc123, section, and description for Lab 4.2
All yes/no inputs use .strip().upper() with no remaining or patterns
load_hazards() returns a list of dictionaries with 'name', 'heads', and 'tails' keys
travel_to() uses D20 rolls and a 30% hazard chance per turn
Final report includes total hazards faced and escaped
All DEBUG/test code has been removed
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–9 only. String methods .strip(), .upper(), and .lower() are the Chapter 8 additions this week. Sets are saved for Module 4.3. CodeGrade will flag code using techniques from later chapters.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–9 (all prior techniques permitted; this week adds .strip(), .upper(), .lower(), and method chaining from Chapter 8, and list of dictionaries with random.choice() from Chapter 9)
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Sets (Module 4.3), list comprehensions, try/except, classes, inheritance

warnings: Do not use sets yet — those are the Module 4.3 reveal. CodeGrade's scope check will flag premature use.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep lab-4-2.py — Module 4.3 adds sets and a visited-cities tracker directly on top of this foundation. You will copy this file and build on it.

ifYouGetStuck: Review the Chapter 8 string methods table in your textbook — .strip(), .upper(), and .lower() are all covered with examples
Test functions in isolation: get load_hazards() working before writing handle_hazard(), and get handle_hazard() working before integrating travel_to()
Check your method chaining order — .strip().upper(), not .strip.upper() (missing parentheses is a common error)
Add DEBUG print statements to see what's happening inside travel_to() — print the roll, miles, and remaining each turn
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
